title: The Narrow Channel of Quantitative Easing: Evidence from YCC Down Under
abstract: We study the recent Australian experience with yield curve control (YCC) as perhaps the best evidence of how this policy might work in other developed economies. YCC seemingly worked well in 2020, when the market expected short rates to stay at zero for a long period of time. As the global recovery and inflation gained momentum in 2021, liftoff expectations moved up, the Reserve Bank of Australia purchased most of the targeted government bond outstanding, and the target bond's yield dislocated from other financial market instruments. The evidence suggests that central bank bond purchase programs can operate more narrowly than previously considered.

article.introduction:
With short-term interest rates at theeffective lower bound during both the Great Recession and the COVID-19 recession, central banks around the world adopted policies of quantitative easing (QE), buying large quantities of government bonds or other similar assets to further ease financial conditions. QE policies can be divided into those that buy a fixed quantity of bonds and those that target, or cap, a particular yield. The latter are collectively known as yield curve control (YCC) policies. Since the mid-1980s, conventional monetary policy in most economies transitioned from targeting monetary (or reserve) aggregates to short-term interest rates. In contrast, nearly all QE programs to date have specified fixed quantities of purchases. An exception to this practice is the policy experiment by the Reserve Bank of Australia (RBA), which, between March 2020 and November 2021, targeted the yield on three-year Australian government bonds (AGBs).
  The Australian YCC experiment is not the first of its kind. Starting in 1942, in an effort to lower the cost of financing war efforts, the Federal Reserve capped interest rates on Treasury securities at 0.375% for bills and at 2.5% for the longest-term Treasury bonds (Toma (1992)). In more recent years, the Bank of Japan adopted a YCC policy in 2016, pegging yields on 10-year Japanese government bonds around zero. The idea of targeting a price has been considered by economists in the United States (Bernanke (2002,2016)) and was noted in the minutes of the June 2020 Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) meeting, but such practice has not been adopted in the United States since the Treasury accord of 1951. The Japanese experience is of limited relevance to the United States because Japan seems so intractably stuck at the zero lower bound, and because the Japanese bond market is dominated by domestic investors (Hoshi and Ito (2012)). Thus, for most other economies, the Australian YCC experiment is the most relevant application in modern times.
  We see the analysis of YCC as informative about the channels through which QE operates. Measuring the effects of quantity-based QE outside the initial announcement windows has been an elusive task, but the transparency of explicit yield targets and maturities in YCC provide direct yardsticks to evaluate QE policies.
  To study the effects of YCC, we first define three sets of channels through which QE operates. “Broad” channels are defined as being ones that affect the prices of all longer-duration assets including those not purchased, operating by means of portfolio-balance channels (Vayanos and Vila (2021)), or signaling channels that convey information about the future path of short-term rates (Bauer and Rudebusch (2014)). These channels combine the effects of beliefs about future short rates and asset demand curves to the extent that the latter apply to all fixed-income assets. In contrast, “narrow channels” are those that impact only the specific class of securities being purchased by the central bank, and “supernarrow” channels are those that impact only the specific security (CUSIP) being bought by the central bank. Narrow channels of QE have been highlighted in prior studies such as Di Maggio, Kermani, and Palmer (2020), Vissing-Jorgensen and Krishnamurthy (2011), Boyarchenko, Fuster, and Lucca (2019), Joyce et al. (2020), and Altavilla, Giannone, and Lenza (2014).
  We then fit smoothed yield curves to AGBs and other fixed-income assets that are close substitutes, and we fit an affine term structure model to decompose yields into expectations and term premium components. These exercises let us separate out broad, narrow, and supernarrow channels, and let us also differentiate signaling and portfolio-balance effects.
  The early experience with YCC appeared to be favorable. The yield on the targeted April 2023 (Apr-23) government security fell nearly 25 basis points (bps) following the (unexpected) policy announcement on March 19, 2020. Essentially all risk-free Australian interest rates at maturities out to a few years were pushed down close to zero. While the RBA bought large amounts of the targeted security in March and April 2020, it largely stopped thereafter as the YCC target was achieved without needing actual purchases. This is not unlike the Federal Reserve historically announcing a target for the funds rate and achieving it with little in the way of open market operations (OMOs; Friedman and Kuttner (2010)).
  However, the spillovers of YCC on other fixed-income assets were already limited in 2020, as evidenced by a 20 bps reduction in the spread between AGBs and other comparable fixed-income instruments such as overnight indexed swaps or bonds issued by Australian states and territories. As the global recovery gained momentum in 2021 and market expectations of an increase in policy interest rates rose, the limited spillovers of YCC on other yields became evident. The RBA purchased substantial quantities of the Apr-24 bond—then the target bond—to achieve the target, ultimately buying the majority of the outstanding security. A kink developed in February 2021 in the AGB yield curve at the Apr-24 maturity. Moreover, the AGB yield curve became disconnected from other short-term interest rates, notably the overnight index swap (OIS) curve and even state government bond yield curves. We estimate the yield response of AGB yields to actual YCC purchase operations and find that the “flow effects” of YCC were narrow, with a sizeable effect on the specific CUSIP but little spillover on other AGBs, OIS expectations, or term premia. These findings together suggest that QE policies ease financial conditions but with an effect that is more limited to the specific assets being purchased than might previously have been hoped (see, e.g., Bernanke (2012,2022)).
  The signaling channel of QE suggests that these policies can successfully reinforce forward guidance. But an affine term structure model indicates that the YCC target became inconsistent with policy expectations in 2021, which suggests that the effect is limited in practice when the stated commitment differs substantially from what market expectations would otherwise be. This implies in turn that YCC cannot change market expectations much. When announcing YCC in March 2020, the RBA provided forward guidance that it would not raise the cash rate target before inflation and employment moved toward its goals. In November 2020, the RBA further stated that it did not expect to achieve the conditions for liftoff for at least three years. Despite this guidance and the accompanying YCC target, once inflation rose, policy expectations moved up.
  We consider a simple multiasset model in the spirit of Vayanos and Vila (2021) to interpret the empirical evidence. We show that YCC shares much of the same features as QE and can operate through both broad and narrow channels. Importantly, however, YCC can also have more limited, supernarrow effects that impact only the specific targeted security, with essentially no spillovers to other maturities or assets.
  The Australian experience is informative about how QE works more generally. When a central bank purchases a fixed quantity of assets, an assessment of the overall effect must examine the effects on the price of that asset and on the prices of close substitutes.1Event-study evidence suggests that central bank asset purchases have large effects on asset prices (Gagnon et al. (2011), Vissing-Jorgensen and Krishnamurthy (2011)). However, an event study only identifies short-term effects—the effects over the long run may be smaller (Wright (2012), Greenlaw et al. (2018), Hanson, Lucca, and Wright (2021)). In contrast, when a central bank targets a price, there is no ambiguity about the impact on the price of the asset being bought, and the question simply becomes whether the price impact is narrow (applying only to the asset class), supernarrow (applying only to the CUSIP being targeted), or broad (applying to other fixed-income asset classes). Before the recovery gained momentum, all short-term interest rates in Australia were close to zero and hence there is no real way to parse the relative importance of a weak economy, forward guidance, or YCC. It is possible that YCC played a role at this point. We find, however, that as the global economic recovery took hold, the price of the bond being targeted became disconnected from the rest of the fixed-income universe. This points to a rather narrow channel for QE more generally. The broad, narrow, and supernarrow channels in our model are also related to the demand system asset pricing approach of Koijen and Yogo (2019), although they do not necessarily imply the same degree of substitutability across asset classes.
  Before concluding, we consider the effects of actual purchase operations on prices of AGBs. Our results are consistent with D'Amico and King's (2013) findings of flow effects of Fed asset purchases as well as with studies on the impact of primary market issuance in Treasury auctions on yields (see, e.g., Duffie (2010); Lou, Yan, and Zhang (2013); Droste, Gorodnichenko, and Ray (2021)).
  The remainder of this paper is organized as follows. In SectionI, we provide background on monetary policy in Australia and describe the timeline of monetary policy events in more detail. SectionIIcontains our main empirical evidence on the effects of YCC. SectionIIIdescribes a simple model for interpreting the Australian experience with YCC. Finally, SectionIVconcludes.
article.I. Monetary Policy in Australia:
The monetary policy decision-making body of the RBA is the Reserve Bank Board, which is composed of the Governor, Deputy Governor, and seven other members. The Reserve Bank Board sets interest rate targets to achieve the objectives set out in the Reserve Bank Act of 1959: a stable currency, full employment, and the economic prosperity and welfare of the people of Australia. Since the early 1990s, these objectives have found practical expression in a target for medium-term consumer price inflation of 2% to 3%, on average.
  In recent decades, the Reserve Bank has set monetary policy by targeting the cash rate, which is an interbank overnight rate. In Australia, most deposits and loans have variable or short-term fixed rates, so there is a high pass-through of changes in these overnight rates to deposit and lending rates. In contrast to the experience in the United States and the Euro area, the cash rate in Australia remained well above the zero lower bound during the Global Financial Crisis. It drifted lower before the COVID-19 pandemic, however, reaching 1.5% in June 2019 and 0.75% in February 2020.
  As the pandemic took hold around the globe in February 2020, and against a backdrop of rapidly deteriorating economic conditions, the Reserve Bank Board introduced a comprehensive set of easing policies, which we summarize in TableI. In March 2020, the Board lowered the cash rate to 0.25% and announced new unprecedented, and “unconventional” monetary policy easing measures as the cash rate effectively hit the zero lower bound. The Reserve Bank implemented a YCC policy by announcing that it would stand ready to purchase AGBs in the secondary market via long-dated OMOs to target a yield of 0.25%, which was set to equate the target for the cash rate. The targeted maturity was the closest to three years: the Apr-23 issue until October 29, 2020, and the Apr-24 issue thereafter. On November 3, 2020, the Board announced that it had lowered both the cash rate and YCC targets to 0.1%. In April 2021, the RBA announced that it would decide whether to roll the target to the next maturity of November 2024. At their July meeting, they decided not to do so and instead continued to apply the target to the Apr-24 bond. By fixing the target to a particular maturity calendar date, the RBA seemingly constructed a strategy to exit from YCC. In the second half of October 2021, however, the targeted bond yield rose well above the target yield, leading the RBA to abandon the strategy on November 2, 2021. YCC policies were paired with forward guidance about the future path of the cash rate as enumerated in TableI.
  The Reserve Bank Board also purchased bonds for reasons other than implementing the YCC target. These purchases included both AGBs and Australian state and territory government securities, commonly referred to as semigovernment securities or “semis.” Starting in March 2020, market functioning (MF) purchases were aimed at restoring market liquidity and reducing market dislocations. These purchases included both AGBs and semis. In November 2020, the Reserve Bank Board announced a more traditional QE program that targeted AGBs and semis in the five- to 10-year sector.2TableIlists the different stages of this program. The RBA publishes data on purchases of AGB securities split into the Bond Purchase Program (only including QE purchases) and long-dated OMOs. Long-dated OMOs include both YCC and MF purchases but do not formally break out their motivation. We consider long-dated OMOs purchasing the Apr-23 or Apr-24 maturities (formal YCC targets) as motivated by YCC; otherwise we treat them as MF purchases. Figures1,2, and3summarize key features of RBA purchases of AGB securities under YCC, QE, and MF categories as described above.
  The orange and green bars in Figure1show the distribution of daily YCC and MF purchases between March 2020 and December 2021. The blue bars in the figure are daily QE purchases starting the day after the QE announcement on November 3, 2020. The figures reveal key differences between purchases under the programs. YCC purchases were irregular in size and ranged from zero on most days to A$3 billion on both February 25 and February 26, 2021. In contrast, QE daily purchases were frequent and essentially always of equal total amount. MF purchases all took place on or before May 4, 2020, except for a small purchase in August 2020. By November 2021, when the YCC program ended, YCC purchases cumulated to about A$23 billion, but only A$8 billion were purchased in 2021. Total YCC purchases accounted for about 4% of total AGBs outstanding by the end of 2021 (Figure2, right panel). In contrast, QE purchases were much larger at about A$200 billion and accounted for about 22% of total AGBs outstanding. As shown in Figure3, by construction YCC applies only to the Apr-23 and Apr-24 issues. By December 2021, these purchases amounted to 61% of the total outstanding amount of the Apr-24 issue. QE purchases had much longer weighted-average maturity.
article.II. Empirical Evidence on the Effect of:
We first present evidence on the impact of YCC, and on the importance of the broad, narrow, and supernarrow transmission channels, using time-series variation in yields of AGBs, OIS, and state-issued bonds (or “semis”). We fit yield curves and examine AGB spreads to OIS and semis. We also decompose AGB and OIS yields using affine term structure models. We then analyze the high-frequency sensitivity of OIS rates to news under YCC in Australia and compare it to New Zealand, where no YCC policies were implemented, as a control. Finally, we examine the high-frequency (daily) responses of AGB yields to RBA purchases. We are interested in studying the effects of YCC, but we explicitly consider MF and QE purchases as well, as the three programs were conducted in parallel.
  Before turning to the empirics, we discuss the broad, narrow, and supernarrow channels in more detail. The model of SectionIIIprovides more formal definitions.
  Broad channels. We consider the OIS rate as having an expectations hypothesis component and, neglecting convexity effects, a remainder term premium component. An asset purchase has a broad effect if it reduces the expectations hypothesis component via signaling. The signaling channel of asset purchases is an indirect channel that affects expectations about the path of future short-term interest rates (Bauer and Rudebusch (2014)). This could work by just informing investors about the likely path of interest rates that the central bank expects, that is communicating a forecast (“Delphic guidance” as in Campbell et al. (2012)), or by stating an actual commitment to a given path of rates (“Odyssean guidance”). Asset purchases also have broad effects if they reduce the term premium. For an asset purchase to have a broad effect, the yields on all fixed-income securities of the same maturity must decline, leaving spreads unchanged. Although our empirical approach focuses on fixed-income assets, what we call a broad channel would also impact nonfixed assets, such as stock prices, through discounting.
  Narrow and supernarrow channels. Asset purchases can also operate via a narrow channel, reducing the yield on the type of security being purchased but not on other comparable assets, thus widening the spread between other assets and the assets being purchased. They can also operate through a supernarrow channel, whereby only the exact security being purchased is affected.3
  We obtain daily data on quoted yields on all AGBs and state-issued bonds from Bloomberg LP. From Barclays we also obtain daily quotes on OIS at maturities of 1, 3, and 6 months and 1, 2, 3, 5, 10, 20, and 30 years. OIS are interest rate swap agreements whereby a fixed rate is swapped against a published index of floating rates. The OIS is just like other fixed- to floating-rate swaps, except that the floating rate is the overnight cash rate rather than the three-month rate typically used in plain-vanilla interest rate swaps.
  We obtain RBA purchases of AGBs from the RBA's release of Monetary Policy Operations (Statistical Table A3) and cash rate targets from Statistical Table A4. We then construct AGB outstanding amounts by CUSIP using AGB issuance data from the Australian Office of Financial Management.
  Yield curve fitting and bootstrapping. We fit separate yield curves at the daily frequency to AGB yields, OIS rates, and the yields on Australian state government bonds. The yield curves are fitted by the well-known functional form of Nelson and Siegel (1987). This is a smooth function that does not allow for sudden yield discontinuities between neighboring maturities. We consider CUSIP-specific fitting errors of individual AGBs relative to this yield curve. This fitting error is closely related to the “noise” measure used by Hu, Pan, and Wang (2013) as a proxy for liquidity. We also use bootstrapping to fit an unsmoothed curve to AGB quotes. The yield curve fitting methods are described in detail in theInternet Appendix.4
  Affine term structure model.
To explore the effects of YCC on expectations of future interest rates and on broader term premia, we fit a three-factor affine term structure model to Australian OIS data and decompose these rates into expected future rates and term premia. We fit our model to OIS rates rather than government bond yields because the latter also reflect liquidity changes induced by both YCC and QE as outlined in the model of SectionIII. Our affine term structure model uses the normalization proposed by Joslin, Singleton, and Zhu (2011), and is estimated at the monthly frequency from November 2009 to December 2021 using OIS rates at maturities of 1, 3, and 6 months and 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, and 10 years. The specification and estimation approach are described in theInternet Appendix. It should be remembered that term premia are not directly observed but rather are estimated from a model and are subject to specification and estimation error.
  The March 19, 2020 announcement that the RBA would target the yield on the Apr-23 AGB at 25 bps came as a surprise. Figure4plots yields on four AGB securities: Apr-23 and Apr-24 which were targeted by YCC (red lines), and the two issues maturing next, that is, Nov-24 and Apr-25, which were not (blue lines). The YCC target bond rolled from the Apr-23 AGB to the Apr-24 AGB in October 2020. As shown by the solid orange line in Figure4, the yield on the Apr-23 bond fell from 48 to 35 bps on that day, dropping further to the 25 bp level over subsequent days. In short order essentially all risk-free Australian interest rates at maturities out to a few years were pushed down close to zero. This can be seen in the top panel of Figure5, which plots the entire term structure of quoted AGB yields (hollow circles) against their duration as well as fitted AGB yields (blue lines) implied by the estimated Nelson-Siegel curve. The figure shows these yield curves on select days in 2020 and 2021.
  Taken in isolation this evidence suggests that YCC was very effective at lowering AGB yields, both those targeted as well as yields of maturities further out. As shown in Figure1, the RBA bought large amounts of three-year AGBs in March and April 2020 and then largely stopped. AGB yields remained low throughout the summer of 2020. Under one interpretation of the evidence, the three-year yield target was credible and thus, for some time after May 2020 the announcement itself was sufficient to accomplish the YCC target without requiring further purchases. This is similar to how the Federal Reserve was historically able to announce a target for the funds rate and achieve it with little need to conduct OMOs (Friedman and Kuttner (2010)). In this respect, YCC appeared to be more effective than standard quantity-based QE programs by keeping yields low even without conducting purchase operations.
  As TableIdemonstrates, however, the RBA implemented YCC on March 19, 2020 along with a portfolio of additional easing measures and, more importantly, lowering the target for the cash rate to 25 bps and providing forward guidance that “[it] will not increase the cash rate until further progress [was made toward its objectives],” which was reinforced in November 2020 with guidance that “[it did] not expect […] to increase the cash rate for at least three years.” As a result, the AGB yield response does not directly tell us the counterfactual of what would have happened if the RBA followed every other policy as it did with the exception of YCC. It is possible that broad channels were at play: YCC purchases could have lowered the term premia via the duration channel (consistent with Vayanos and Vila (2021)) and reinforced forward guidance via the signaling channel (as in Bauer and Rudebusch (2014)). But limited YCC purchases in 2020 were also consistent with a scenario in which the YCC target is equal to market expectations about the path of short-term rates and in essence playing no role.
  AGB yields continued to seemingly respond to YCC policies in the fall of 2020. First, as the RBA rolled the target bond from the Apr-23 to the Apr-24 AGB issue on October 20, 2020, the two yields converged (top panel of Figure4, solid and dashed orange lines). Second, the targeted AGB yields declined to 10 bps as the YCC (and cash rate) target were lowered on November 3, 2020. Similar to earlier in 2020, the lower YCC target was achieved with limited purchases. The OIS rate with the same duration as the Apr-24 AGB issue was close to zero in 2020 and its expectations hypothesis component was also near zero.5
  However, as the global recovery gained momentum in early 2021, expectations for liftoff of policy interest rates over the next year rose. This can be seen by the increase in the dashed blue line in the bottom panel of Figure4. This corresponds to the expectations hypothesis OIS component of maturity equal to the duration of the targeted bond, as implied by the affine term structure model. The increase in policy expectations occurred over the second half of February. There was no single trigger for this increase, but higher-than-expected wage data were released in Australia at this time. In addition, U.S. inflation data and progress on COVID vaccinations raised monetary policy expectations in both the United States and abroad. As policy expectations firmed, the RBA purchased large quantities of the Apr-24 AGB issue and by late 2021 it owned the majority of the balance outstanding as shown in Figures1and3.
  Over the summer of 2021, the yields on YCC issues (orange line in the top panel of Figure4) remained at the target but were disconnected from other interest rates. The Apr-24 yield became disconnected from the duration-matched OIS (bottom panel of this figure). The widening of this spread is consistent with the narrow channel. Yields on the Apr-24 AGB were above duration-matched OIS in 2020, leading to a negative swap spread, likely owing to the balance sheet costs of holding bonds (Boyarchenko et al. (2018), Jermann (2020), Du, Hébert, and Li (2022)). This swap spread then flipped, becoming positive around the end of 2020. The increase in OIS rates relative to their AGB counterparts is also consistent with Joyce et al. (2020), who study the effects of the Bank of England's QE program on Gilt-OIS spreads and find evidence that Gilt purchases resulted in the widening of spreads.
  Furthermore, and specific to YCC, the Apr-24 yield also decoupled from AGB yields of slightly longer maturities as shown by the blue line in Figure4. Issues maturing in November 2024 and April 2025 essentially act as a YCC control group. As shown in the bottom left panel of Figure5, a kink developed in the government bond yield curve at the Apr-24 maturity as evidenced by the orange circles in the figure. This kink in the yield curve is highly unusual because it suggests an arbitrage opportunity consisting of shorting the Apr-24 issue, which trades rich, and buying the Nov-25 issue. This anomalous pricing is even more evident when comparing the term structure of bootstrapped unsmoothed forward rates. As seen in the bottom panels of Figure6, starting in early 2021 a large discontinuity opened up around the three-year sector, with forward rates jumping about 1.5% around the YCC target maturity. This is what we would expect from the supernarrow channel when the YCC target is too high relative to the expected path of short-term interest rates. Yields on the targeted AGB detach from swap rates, a kink develops in the yield curve, and the central bank ends up holding much of the outstanding securities.
  As seen in Figure4, on October 27, 2021, after Australian consumer price data came in well above expectations, the kink in the yield curve became even more pronounced, but the yield on the Apr-24 issue itself began to move away from the target. The RBA conducted one large operation (Figure1) but did not intervene in the following days as the yield on the Apr-24 issue rose further. The YCC policy was formally discontinued on November 3, 2021.
  While not the main focus in this paper, it is worth noting that the estimated term premium component of the duration-matched OIS rate fell in October 2020, as can be seen in the difference between the solid and dashed blue lines in the bottom panel of Figure4. This happened around the time of Governor Lowe's speech foreshadowing a more conventional QE program. The program ended up being very big, buying A$4 billion per week in AGBs concentrated in the five- to 10-year maturity range. In October 2020, the estimated term premium component of OIS rates went from being positive to around zero, and in early 2021 it went negative. This pattern is consistent with the QE program operating in part through term premia—a broad channel when purchases are very large. After the collapse of the YCC program, the OIS term premium reverted to being positive, suggesting that this effect was ultimately not persistent. Finlay, Titkov, and Xiang (2021) estimate that the QE program lowered longer-term AGB yields by about 30 bps.
  The total purchases of the Apr-2024 bond from October 2020 to October 2021 were A$13.9 billion. Over this period, the Apr-2024 issue averaged about 20 bps rich to the OIS curve, and its duration was about three years. A back-of-the-envelope calculation indicates that had the RBA bought these securities at the yield implied by the OIS curve, they would have paid about A$83 million less. This could underestimate the amount that the RBA “overpaid” because (i) bond yields before March 2020 were typically above OIS rates in Australia, and (ii) the RBA also bought substitute bonds in defense of the target, although they were only slightly rich to the OIS curve. A calculation such as this measures the ex ante cost of the purchases. The actual ex post cost will depend on how the yield on the purchased securities compares to any interest that the RBA will have to pay on reserves, for the purpose of managing short rates, up to the point that these securities mature.
  While we cannot fully identify the counterfactual of what would have happened if the RBA had omitted the yield curve target, consistent with the prediction of the model in SectionIII, the gravitational pull of the AGB yield curve on other short-term interest rates was limited, which became evident as the expected path of short-term rates detached from the YCC AGB target. This outcome appears more along the lines described by the irrelevance proposition of OMOs of Eggertsson and Woodford (2003), as the RBA bought up the targeted bond but achieved little else. This outcome was also foreseen by Bernanke (2016), who wrote that the central bank “could end up owning most or all of the eligible securities, with uncertain consequences for interest rates overall.” The outcome is similar to when the Fed capped interest rates during and immediately after World War II, although the recent Australian experience involved acquiring most of the stock of the targeted asset much faster.
  The narrow channel of YCC implies that purchases of AGBs should be associated with a widening of the spread of other safe fixed-income instruments relative to AGBs. The bottom panel of Figure4shows that the Apr-24 yield became disconnected from the duration-matched OIS in 2021. Here, we present additional evidence using semigovernment bonds, or “semis,” which are issued by Australian states and territories. Semis have a guarantee from the state government and in some cases a federal guarantee. Semis should thus be close substitutes for federal bonds. The comparison between Australian federal and state bonds gives an indication of spillovers of YCC policies on the highest-quality bonds, potentially comparable to spillovers from U.S. Treasury yields onto agency mortgage-backed securities or AAA corporate bond yields.
  We fit Nelson-Siegel zero-coupon curves to state bond yields separately for each state using bonds with maturities less than 10 years. Australia has six states and two territories, but we omit Tasmania and both territories because they have few bonds, leaving us with five state yield curves that are all very close to each other. We then take the spread between the Apr-24 federal bond yield and the duration-matched zero-coupon yield from each state's yield curve. This is shown in Figure7(red lines), where the thick line is the average across the five state yields. The blue lines repeat the exercise for the Apr-26 bond, which serves as a control, with the motivation being that the Apr-26 bond is clearly outside the RBA's target maturity. We see that semi spreads to AGBs widened sharply in March 2020 due to global flight to quality, and this liquidity event persisted through May 2020. The liquidity episode affected state bonds across maturities, and indeed spreads in the Apr-24 and Apr-26 bonds comoved during this episode (red and blue lines). While we focus in this paper on the effects of RBA purchases on AGBs, both MF and QE purchases included semis as well, with 20% of QE purchases allotted to semis. These purchases seemingly pushed semi spreads to AGBs to low levels in 2020. However, as the YCC three-year target diverged from market expectations for the cash rate starting in 2021, semi spreads on the Apr-24 AGB widened significantly and decoupled from the Apr-26 AGB spread. The duration-matched semi spread to the Apr-24 AGB yield averaged 21 bps from the inception of YCC to January 31, 2021, and the semi spread to the Apr-26 AGB yield also averaged 21 bps over this period. However, from February 1, 2021 to the collapse of YCC on November 2, 2021, these spreads averaged 28 and 10 bps, respectively. They peaked at 68 and 13 bps, respectively, near the end of October 2021. In conclusion, when YCC became inconsistent with expectations for the cash rate, the richness of the targeted three-year bond did not spill over to similar-maturity state bonds, even though state bonds were also being purchased by the RBA. These results provide further evidence consistent with the supernarrow channel.
  The time-series evidence shows that, consistent with the supernarrow channel, when the YCC is inconsistent with policy expectations, yields on the targeted AGB detach from other rates. The broad signaling channel of YCC instead suggests that YCC is a policy commitment that spills over onto market expectations for the path of short-term interest rates.
  Over the 2015 to 2019 period, we see that both Australia and New Zealand exhibited similar sensitivity to U.S. OIS rates. In the April to December 2020 period, the sensitivity of Australian OIS rates declined specifically around the three-year maturity, while there was no such pattern for New Zealand. In 2021, both Australia and New Zealand exhibited similar sensitivity to U.S. OIS rates. This is again consistent with the pattern of the RBA having been successful with forward guidance in 2020, and with YCC possibly contributing to this success while the target for YCC was consistent with the expected cash rate. But once YCC guidance diverged from the expected path of the cash rate, it ceased to have much impact. We discuss these scenarios in detail in the model of SectionIII.
  The RBA preannounced YCC and QE purchases just like other foreign central banks had done during the Great Recession and the COVID-19 recession. Most asset pricing models, including the one presented in SectionIII, in which forward-looking investors price financial assets in terms of future prices and cash flows imply that asset prices respond to credible purchase announcements even if no actual purchase has occurred. In fact, as shown in the time-series evidence, AGB yields responded to both YCC and QE announcements, consistent with prior studies on the effects of central bank QE announcements (Gagnon et al. (2011), Vissing-Jorgensen and Krishnamurthy (2011)). But existing studies also show that central bank transactions have a high-frequency impact on price. For example, D'Amico and King (2013) study the security-level effects of Federal Reserve large-scale asset purchases and find significant impacts. Others study the closely related impact of primary market issuance in Treasury auctions on yields (see, e.g., Duffie (2010), Lou, Yan, and Zhang (2013), Droste, Gorodnichenko, and Ray (2021)), which despite being known well in advance affect secondary market Treasury yields. In this section, we study how daily RBA purchase operations affected yields on AGBs and on OIS. We also examine whether narrow, supernarrow, or broad channels were most important.
  Results. TableIIreports coefficient estimates of linear regressions of changes in AGB yields and duration-matched OIS rates (equation (2)). AGB yields and matched OIS rates are not statistically significantly affected by total YCC purchases. The effect of total MF and YCC purchases on AGB yields are both significantly negative (at the 10% level or better), but the effects on matched OIS rates are not significant. When we look at purchases of the CUSIP over which the AGB yield change is defined, we see that same-CUSIP purchases show statistically significant negative effects for both YCC and QE purchases on the AGB yields (column (3)) but not on matched-maturity OIS rates (column (4)). An A$1 billion purchase under the YCC program lowers the yield of the specific AGB by 2 bps (p-value<$<$0.1), while purchases under the QE program lower the yield by about half as much (1 bp,p-val<$<$0.05). These estimated effects are economically large. For example, as shown in right panel of Figure3, the RBA purchased a cumulative A$20 billion of the Apr-24 issue via the YCC program. The point estimates suggest that these purchases lowered the yield on the AGB Apr-24 issue by about 40 bps. We find no effects of either total or specific-CUSIP purchases on OIS rates suggesting that broad channels were not present across the three programs. We find some evidence that QE and especially MF purchases affected yields for all AGBs. For YCC, we only find effects for CUSIP-specific purchases. In other words, the supernarrow channel of YCC was an important transmission channel not only as QE/YCC policies were announced and when the bulk of price impact was realized (see SectionII.B), but also as the RBA conducted its purchase operations.
  Robustness.
  A concern with the analysis above is that the point estimates may be driven by a few outlier observations. There were YCC purchases on March 20, 2020, the day after the program was announced. As a result, the estimated YCC effect may be overly influenced by this observation if the effect of the YCC announcement carried over to the following day. Columns (5) to (8) of TableIIrepeat the estimation excluding this observation from the sample. As in the baseline specification, YCC total AGB purchases do not have statistically significant effects on yields. For same-CUSIP purchases, the estimated YCC yield effect increases from 2 to 2.3 bps.
  An additional concern with the estimated yield effects is related to the fact that the AGB purchases may have not been randomly assigned. In theInternet Appendix, we estimate the RBA's AGB purchase rules. We conclude that the exact timing and size of purchases were hard to predict across the three purchase programs. So while purchases followed the decision of the RBA, the exact timing and size of a given CUSIP were difficult to predict. When we include all controls from the purchase rules estimated in theInternet Appendixin column (9) of TableII, we consistently find that the estimated coefficients are largely unaffected, although the coefficient on QE-CUSIP loses significance.
  Decomposition of the estimated yield effects.
We next use the yield curve and affine term structure decomposition to assess which channels may be important in determining the high-frequency response of yield changes on asset purchases. Here we focus on the effects of purchases of a specific CUSIP. We first note that the response of the duration-matched OIS in TableIIis negative but never significant, suggesting no broad channels of flow purchases. TableIIIsplits the change in the spread of the AGB yield over the duration-matched OIS into two components: (i) the AGB fitting error, that is, the difference between the actual AGB yield and the fitted AGB yield from the Nelson-Siegel curve,6and (ii) the spread between the AGB and OIS yield curves. We can see in columns (2) and (3) of the table that for the YCC-CUSIP purchases, much of the response is due to the AGB fitting error, while the effect of QE purchases depends on both the fitting error and the spread between the fitted AGB yield and the OIS, although only the former is statistically significant. In other words, the YCC program appears to have had a CUSIP-specific effect with little spillover onto other CUSIPs, as indicated by the stability of the yield curve, consistent with the narrowest possible channel. When we exclude the March 20, 2020 observations (columns (5) to (8)) the effects are very similar.
  We next use the affine term structure model to decompose the OIS yield change into expectation and term premium components. The results are reported in TableIV. While negative, the effect on the OIS yield is never statistically significant. We similarly find the effects on the two components to be small in magnitude and not statistically significant. Recall that as the QE program was announced, term premia did fall (see the bottom panel of Figure4), but here we do not find that the actual purchases significantly affected term premia.
  In sum, the high-frequency evidence further suggests that the flow effects of YCC were supernarrow, with a sizeable effect on the specific CUSIP but little spillover onto other AGBs (narrow channel), OIS expectations, or term premia as indicated by the term premium decomposition (broad channel).
article.III. Model:
Model assumptions. We consider a simple model in the spirit of Vayanos and Vila (2021) and Greenwood, Hanson, and Stein (2010) to interpret the empirical evidence in the previous section. We use the model to demonstrate the different channels through which YCC affects yields on AGBs targeted by YCC and spillovers on longer-dated AGBs and swaps. We also use the model to illustrate differences between YCC and unconventional quantity-based policies. The first key takeaway of the model is that YCC shares much of the same features as unconventional quantity-based purchase programs. YCC affects yields via a portfolio-balance channel, a signaling channel, and an asset-specific liquidity channel. We next find that the portfolio-balance and signaling channels are broad channels that have similar effects across all fixed-income securities, directly targeted or not, with loadings that depend on the security's duration. In contrast, the asset-specific liquidity effect is a narrow channel that is specific to the asset class targeted by YCC. Finally, when the YCC policy is inconsistent with the path of short-term rates or yields on other comparable-maturity assets, YCC has narrow effects on the targeted AGB with no spillovers onto other assets. This supernarrow channel, which was prevalent during much of 2021, is not shared with quantity-based policies.
  There are four periods:t$t$= 0, 1, 2, and 3. Short-term interest rates are exogenous and determined by monetary policy through an infinitely elastic storage technology. The short-term rate from period 0 to 1, denoted byr1$r_{1}$, is known in period 0. Short-term rates from 1 to 2, denoted byr2$r_{2}$, and from period 2 to 3, denoted byr3$r_{3}$, are unknown at time 0, have meanμt$\mu _{t}$and varianceσt2$\sigma _{t}^{2}$fort=2,3$t=2,3$, and are independent of each other. In addition, default-free two- and three-period bondsB(2)$B_{(2)}$andB(3)$B_{(3)}$exist that pay one unit of wealth at in periods 2 and 3, and that trade in period 0 with prices ofPB(2)$P^{B_{(2)}}$andPB(3)$P^{B_{(3)}}$, respectively. In contrast to the short-term rate technology, the supply of these bonds is fixed, and their prices are determined endogenously and reflect unconventional policies. Given period-0 prices, yields on the bond areYB(i)≡(PB(i))−i−1$Y^{B_{(i)}}\equiv (P^{B_{(i)}})^{-i}-1$for maturitiesi=2,3$i=2,3$. We primarily study the effects of unconventional policies on the two-period bond. We also include the three-period bond in the model to study cross-maturity spillovers. To understand spillovers on other assets, the model also features two- and three-period swapsS(2)$S_{(2)}$andS(3)$S_{(3)}$that pay one unit of wealth in periods 2 and 3 and trade in period 0 with pricesPS(2)$P^{S_{(2)}}$andPS(3)$P^{S_{(3)}}$, respectively, which are also determined endogenously. We need period 1 to examine the possibility of a kink in the yield curve in period 1, as we see in the data. Without period 1, we could only show a flatter/steeper yield curve, which would not correspond to a kink in the yield curve.
  Payoffs of bonds and swaps with the same maturities are identical but their market price can differ reflecting their relative supply and the fact that investors face other nonpecuniary costs to taking positions in these assets. Such costs include liquidity and transaction costs as well as balance sheet costs resulting from regulation or leverage constraints. In the United States, for example, since the Great Financial Crisis, regulatory reforms to boost macrofinancial stability impose constraints on dealers' balance sheet capacity (Duffie (2018)). The supplemental leverage ratio (SLR) introduced with the Basel III regulatory framework constrains banks' leverage—especially for globally systemically important banks. Other limitations on proprietary trading (such as the Volcker rule) and the liquidity coverage ratio (LCR) similarly increase the cost of holding positions in fixed-income assets, even if default-free. Moreover, the effective balance sheet costs can be greater for government bonds than for swaps and thus can drive swap spreads as argued by Jermann (2020), Du, Hébert, and Li (2022), and Boyarchenko et al. (2018).
  There are four agents: preferred-habitat investors, arbitrageurs, the government, and the central bank. In period 0, preferred-habitat investors demand quantityH(i)$H^{_{(i)}}$of thei$i$-period assets fori=2,3$i=2,3$. We assume thatH(i)$H^{(i)}$is all invested inB(i)$B_{(i)}$ifYS(i)−YB(i)≤κ$Y^{S_{(i)}}-Y^{B_{(i)}}\le \kappa$; otherwise,H(i)$H^{(i)}$is all invested inS(i)$S_{(i)}$. Thus, the preferred-habitat investors are inelastic across maturity, but their demand functions have some elasticity within maturity consistent with the downward-sloping demand curves of Vayanos and Vila (2021).7
  The central bank can affectPB(2)$P^{B_{(2)}}$via unconventional monetary policy by purchasing quantityCB(2)$C^{B_{(2)}}$of the two-period bondB(2)$B_{(2)}$. We assume that the central bank does not transact in the swapS(2)$S_{(2)}$. We define YCC as a program that sets the yield on the two-period bondYB(2)$Y^{B_{(2)}}$to a targetY∗$Y^{*}$.
  The government issues a dollar quantity of bondsQB(i)$Q^{B_{(i)}}$fori=2,3$i=2,3$, and the swapS(i)$S_{(i)}$fori=2,3$i=2,3$is in zero net supply. This is without loss of generality because solving the arbitrageur's optimization only requires that we keep track of the period-0 excess supply net of preferred-habitat quantities demanded. LetqB(i)=QB(i)−H(i)×I(YS(i)−YB(i)≤κ)$q^{B_{(i)}}=Q^{B_{(i)}}-H^{_{(i)}}\times \mathbb {I}(Y^{S_{(i)}}-Y^{B_{(i)}}\le \kappa)$andqS(i)=−H(i)×I(YS(i)−YB(i)>κ)$q^{S_{(i)}}=-H^{_{(i)}}\times \mathbb {I}(Y^{S_{(i)}}-Y^{B_{(i)}}>\kappa)$be the residual supplies to arbitrageurs net of the preferred-habitat quantities demanded.
  Arbitrageurs hold a totalqB(2)−CB(2)$q^{B_{(2)}}-C^{B_{(2)}}$of bondsB(2)$B_{(2)}$. We assume that this quantity cannot be negative because the central bank does not allow shorting. This is consistent with the experience in Australia as the RBA limited the extent to which investors could borrow, and thus short, securities from its portfolio. IfCB(2)=qB(2)$C^{B_{(2)}}=q^{B_{(2)}}$, then the central bank buys the full amount not wanted by the preferred-habitat investors and pays a price corresponding toY*$Y^{\ast}$. In contrast, the arbitrageurs' holdings of the swap can be either positive or negative, and they match the position of the preferred-habitat investors because they are in zero net supply.
  As in Vayanos and Vila (2021), arbitrageurs are risk-averse and have limited risk-bearing capacity. We depart from their specification in also assuming that arbitrageurs face additional asset-specific nonpecuniary costs to investing. Arbitrageurs finance their positions by borrowing short term. They have risk toleranceγ$\gamma$and nonpecuniary marginal costsθB$\theta _{B}$andθS$\theta _{S}$to taking positionsaB(i)$a^{B_{(i)}}$andaS(i)$a^{S_{(i)}}$. Arbitrageurs start with zero wealth,w0=0$w_{0}=0$, and chooseaS(i)$a^{S_{(i)}}$andaB(i)$a^{B_{(i)}}$to maximize mean-variance preferences of wealth in period 2,w2$w_{2}$, net of balance sheet and transaction costs.8
  As noted above, in the model trading is only allowed in period 0. We could allow for some types of trading in later periods. For example, the arbitrageurs could trade among themselves in period 1. However, because they are all identical, there will be no trade in equilibrium and thus such trade will have no implications for period-2 wealth or the solution of the model in period 0.
  The first-order conditions foraS(i)$a^{S_{(i)}}$(formulas omitted) are analogs to (5). The left-hand side of the two first-order conditions are the expected two-period excess return over the expected short-term funding cost forB(2)$B_{(2)}$andB(3)$B_{(3)}$. The right-hand side is the marginal cost to investing in the bond. The first term is the increase in the portfolio exposure to the volatility inr2$r_{2}$scaled by the size of the portfolioV$V$and the risk-aversion parameterγ$\gamma$. This effect is prominent in Vayanos and Vila (2021) and measures the duration risk to investing in long-term assets. The second term is the increase in the nonpecuniary cost of holding additional bonds on the balance sheet. The formula for the three-period bond shows that, in addition, the marginal cost to investing inB(3)$B_{(3)}$includes a term that reflects duration exposure tor3$r_{3}$.
  Broad channel. The broad channels consist of both signaling and term premium channels. The signaling channel works by loweringμ2$\mu _{2}$. The term premium channel affects the two-year bond yield spread,(1+YB(2))2−(1+r1)(1+μ2)$(1+Y^{B_{(2)}})^2-(1+r_{1})(1+\mu _{2})$, by lowering interest rate risk,σ22$\sigma _{2}^{2}$. Either effect is broad across all yields because it lowers the yield onB(2)$B_{(2)}$over the path of short rates while leaving the swap spread and term spreads unaffected.
  Narrow and supernarrow channels. Central bank purchases also operate with a narrow channel as they lower the cost of carrying the bond on the balance sheet as measured byθB$\theta _{B}$. As shown in (6), the liquidity channel is narrow as it only affects bonds, not swaps. In other words, asset purchases lower the spread of bonds to the expected path of short rates but leave swap yields unaffected.
  Calibration. We provide three calibrations of the models that are useful to interpret the empirical evidence and show how the yield configuration in 2020 to 2021 can emerge. The calibrations can result in either interior or corner solutions. Across the three calibrations, we setr1=0,QB(2)=QB(3)=1$r_{1}=0,Q^{B_{(2)}}=Q^{B_{(3)}}=1$,QS(2)=QS(3)=0$Q^{S_{(2)}}=Q^{S_{(3)}}=0$,H(2)=H(3)=0.5$H^{(2)}=H^{(3)}=0.5$,θB=0.001$\theta _{B}=0.001$,θS=0$\theta _{S}=0$,κ=0.002$\kappa =0.002$, andγ=0.5$\gamma =0.5$in all cases. Furthermore, in all cases the central bank adopts a 10 bp YCC, orY*=0.001$Y^{\ast}=0.001$, buying whatever amount is needed to enforce this target, which could amount to a corner solution. The three scenarios differ in the choice of the meanμi$\mu _i$and standard deviationσi$\sigma _i$ofri$r_i$fori=2,3$i=2,3$as shown (in bps) in TableV.
  The first parameter configuration is intended to be one in which the YCC target does not clash with expectations for the path of the cash rate. The expected short rate is 10 bps until the YCC target bond matures, although there is some uncertainty about the path of short rates. This is intended to illustrate the situation in Australia in 2020.
  The second parameter configuration is one in which the YCC target is slightly inconsistent with the expected path of policy. This is because expected short rates over the time until the YCC target bond matures average 30 bps, and there is uncertainty about this path. This is intended to illustrate the Australian situation in early 2021.
  The third parameter configuration is one in which there is a more dramatic inconsistency between the YCC target and rate expectations, as well as more uncertainty about the path of short rates. This is intended to illustrate the Australian situation shortly before the collapse of YCC in late 2021.
  Results. Figure9shows the resulting asset yields in the three cases. In the first parameter configuration, the YCC target (Y*=μ2>r1)$Y^{\ast}=\mu _2>r_1)$is close to what would be obtained without any central bank purchases, and the central bank buys only 0.014 units ofB(2)$B_{(2)}$. The YCC target does not have a material effect here unless we think of its existence as lowering future expected short rates (μ2$\mu _2$andμ3$\mu _3$), which would be a broad signaling channel, or unless its existence could have lowered uncertainty about future short rates, which we would again think of as a broad channel.
  In the second parameter configuration,YB(2)$Y^{B_{(2)}}$is disconnected from the other two-period asset and also from the three-period bond, leading to a kink in the bond yield curve. The central bank achieves the YCC target, but only by buying up all of the two-period bond that is not demanded by the preferred-habitat investors; the central bank buys 0.5 units of this bond. Since the yield on the three-period bond and the other asset are little changed, we consider this scenario as consistent with the supernarrow channel of transmission.
  In the third parameter configuration, the two-period bond is again disconnected from all other assets, but now the gap betweenYB(2)$Y^{B_{(2)}}$andYS(2)$Y^{S_{(2)}}$is so big that the preferred-habitat investors sell all of their holdings ofB(2)$B_{(2)}$and instead demand the other two-period asset,S(2)$S_{(2)}$. The central bank now has to absorb the entire supply of the two-period bond. This scenario is consistent with the supernarrow channel.
  Note that only because short selling is forbidden do we end up in a corner solution in the second and third configurations. If arbitrageurs could take an arbitrarily large short position, then there would still be an interior solution.
article.IV. Conclusions:
The Australian experience with YCC is an extremely valuable test case for how such policies may work in other developed economies. We conclude that while YCC may have eased financial conditions when in 2020 investors expected short-term rates to remain low for an extended period, once the YCC target became inconsistent with the expected path of short-term rates in 2021, the transmission of YCC became supernarrow to the CUSIP being targeted, with little or no easing in broader financial conditions. Consistent with our model prediction, the RBA purchased most of the targeted AGB issues with no spillover onto policy expectations (both the OIS and its expectations-hypothesis component), longer-dated AGBs, or close-substitute instruments such as state-issued bonds.
  This outcome of a super-narrow channel is along the lines of the irrelevance proposition of OMOs of Eggertsson and Woodford (2003). The RBA bought up the targeted bond but achieved little else. It also resembles the experience with YCC in the United States during World War II when the Fed capped Treasury bill rates at 0.375% and over time ended up accumulating most of the outstanding T-bills, reaching 75% of the total amount outstanding in 1945 (Garbade (2020)). The Federal Reserve gave up the T-bill rate cap in 1947, and then began purchasing large quantities of long-term bonds as expectations for short-term rates rose. However, the Australian YCC experience involved a much faster accumulation of the targeted asset. YCC may work when it is roughly consistent with market expectations, but it does not appear to be successful in holding down the term structure of interest rates other than the targeted security when market participants see the central bank as likely to begin raising short-term interest rates. It is important to bear in mind that the RBA restricted short selling. Had they allowed unbounded short selling, they would have taken a much bigger risk of a large capital loss with the consequent costs for the independence of the central bank, but that may in turn have made the yield target more credible in equilibrium.
  We think that the Australian YCC experiment is informative about quantity-based QE programs as well. The prominent role of narrow liquidity channels suggests that broader channels, such as portfolio-balance and duration effects (Tobin (1969), Vayanos and Vila (2021)) or signaling about the path of short-term rates (Bauer and Rudebusch (2014)) have little sustained impact. We find that YCC operated largely through a supernarrow channel whereby only the specific asset being bought was impacted, with no spillovers elsewhere. We also find some narrow effects on the class of asset being bought beyond the specific security, which can ease financial conditions for the targeted assets, but these narrow effects do not spill over onto other financial assets and thus the composition of central bank operations becomes crucial. In the U.S. context, the results would imply that Treasury purchases do not have much potential to spill over onto mortgage and corporate rates. Purchases of mortgage backed security (MBS) are likely to have more impact on mortgage rates. And while the Federal Reserve Act does not allow the Fed to buy corporate securities in OMOs, facilities to support financing of corporate bonds, as implemented in 2020, may be effective in lowering yields on this asset class, but only because this asset class is being targeted. This implication is consistent with Boyarchenko, Kovner, and Shachar (2022), who find that supernarrow effects operated in 2020 as the Fed corporate bond purchases largely eased financing for facility-eligible issuers.
  Editors: Antoinette Schoar, Urban Jermann, Leonid Kogan, Jonathan Lewellen, and Thomas Philippon
